superseded_reason: Hostile review #1 found 3 critical + 4 major + 3 moderate issues (patch byte-exactness, broken env-var toggle, soft-fallback reuse, operator-push enforcement inversion, curmudgeon-side queue_id write-through, retroactive audit rigor, FUSE/clone race). PROP-009r addressed most and was itself superseded by PROP-009r2 after hostile review #2 found a shell-quote bug, missing ownership-triad entries, and a mtime factual error. Applied via commits 314904e (step 1) and 0f2dc79 (step 3).
target: monitor/prompts/decider.md (Step E2) and monitor/prompts/curmudgeon.md (Step 0b Procedure line 2)
operator_directive: DIRECTIVE-20260418-002 — Diagnose decider draining post-integration re-review queue items without curmudgeon review files
problem: The decider pops items from priority-queue.json using substring match against the reviews/ directory, and the curmudgeon skips items from the queue using the identical substring match. Neither consults queue_id or compares reviewed_at against pushed_at. Any time a target_id that has been reviewed before (e.g., SEC-4.1, WIN-058) is re-pushed to the queue as a post-integration re-review, BOTH sides' substring matches succeed instantly against the pre-existing old review files. Result: the decider pops the new push as if it had been reviewed, and the curmudgeon never picks it up because it sees it as already-reviewed. Post-integration adversarial review is silently skipped. The bug has the same root cause on both sides — the pair is a symmetric pre-condition: 'if any file in reviews/ contains the target_id substring, the item is done.' This is not what 'done' should mean.

problem_evidence.operator_window: 2026-04-18T07:26Z (commit 2fa4b57 → 29e11ac) and 2026-04-18T09:34Z (pushed_at) → 09:41Z (commit e4630ec). Ten queue items (qid 165-174, all post-EXP-181/EXP-182 integration re-reviews) were pushed and popped inside the same decider run with no matching curmudgeon review files produced.
problem_evidence.decider_bug_location: monitor/prompts/decider.md lines 194-205 (Step E2 — Pop reviewed items). The pop filter does: `const hasReview=reviews.some(f=>searchTerms.some(t=>f.includes(t)));` against every file in the reviews directory. No queue_id check, no reviewed_at vs pushed_at check.
problem_evidence.curmudgeon_mirror_bug_location: monitor/prompts/curmudgeon.md Step 0b procedure line 2 (around line 89): 'Check if any filename contains the item's target_id.' — identical substring match, same blind spot.

affected_queue_entries_verified.matching_review_file: none with queue_id=165; substring-matched against pre-existing SEC-4.1.c2/c3/c4.json (c3 tagged queue_id 158, c4 tagged queue_id 163 — neither covers 165)
none with queue_id=166; only WIN-058.json (Apr 08) and WIN-058.c2.json (Apr 08) exist
none with queue_id=167; WIN-058b-post-EXP102.c2.json is tagged queue_id 86 (pre-dates qid 167 by ~80 items)
none with queue_id=168; only old WIN-062.json (Apr 08) and WIN-062.c2.json (Apr 08)
WIN-053.c3.json reviewed_at 2026-04-18T06:13:49.900Z — queue_id field missing; reviewed_at pre-dates the ~07:26Z push by 73 min, so even a reviewed_at > pushed_at rule would NOT match
none with queue_id=170; SEC-2b.5.c1 (qid 155) and SEC-2b.5-post-apr18.c1 (qid 161) both pre-date qid 170
same as qid 165 — no matching queue_id, and nothing reviewed after 09:34Z

problem_evidence.impact_confirmed: SEC-4.1 post-EXP-182 (a major fold-in rewrite with a 47.69σ independence-violation wedge), WIN-058 + WIN-062 post-EXP-181 (the kappa-badge recompute), and SEC-2b.5 went live with no adversarial curmudgeon check. Both post-integration review passes (07:26Z and 09:41Z) were silently skipped. If EXP-183 and EXP-184 follow the same path after their pre-integration reviews clear, we will lose both pre-integration AND post-integration adversarial checks — full loss of coverage on expansions.

hypothesis_ruling.H1_integration_equals_reviewed: PARTIALLY — not by explicit rule but as a side effect. Decider.md has no rule 'if just integrated then pop'; the pop happens because pre-existing old reviews substring-match the target_id. So the symptom is 'integration items auto-pop' but the mechanism is the substring match, not intentional short-circuit.
hypothesis_ruling.H2_dedup_auto_drain: RULED OUT — decider.md has no dedup-based auto-drain. The push-side dedup rule (line 283) prevents pushing duplicates into the queue, but does not pop existing items. No batch-drain on duplicates.
hypothesis_ruling.H3_curmudgeon_stopped_at_change_detectio: PARTIALLY — curmudgeon probably never reached qid 165-174 because its Step 0b line 2 substring match incorrectly flagged them as already-reviewed (mirror bug). The stale-state echo SEC-4.1.c3.json at 07:42Z tagged queue_id 158 is separate and caused by cycle-numbering race (see orthogonal finding below), not by this hypothesis.
hypothesis_ruling.H4_batch_drain_queue_management: RULED OUT — commit e4630ec ('queue management') does pop five items in one shot, but it is the normal Step E2 filter popping all items whose target_id substring-matches, not a deliberate batch-drain. There is no code path in decider.md that says 'pop all items at once'. The batch effect is emergent from N items all substring-matching on one run.

unmatched_pops_ledger_since_2026_04_15.method: Cross-check every history entry popped_at >= 2026-04-15 against monitor/curmudgeon/reviews/ for a review file whose queue_id field equals the popped queue_id. Entries without a queue_id match are candidates; some are benign (pre-schema reviews without queue_id tags whose reviewed_at post-dates the pushed_at), others are confirmed bugs.
unmatched_pops_ledger_since_2026_04_15.history_capacity_limitation: pq.history is capped at 50 entries (decider.md line 206 `slice(-50)`). Entries older than qid 145 (popped 2026-04-16T03:43Z) have been truncated from the queue file, so a full 2026-04-15+ ledger reconstruction requires git log -p on priority-queue.json. This PROP focuses on the confirmed 10-item window; a full sweep would be a follow-up task for tinker.
unmatched_pops_ledger_since_2026_04_15.confirmed_or_likely_unreviewed_pops_pre_: Unknowable from current history capacity without git-archaeology. The 2026-04-18T05:32:54Z triple-pop (qids 162 WIN-032, 163 SEC-4.1, 164 SEC-2b.5) — qid 163 has a matching SEC-4.1.c4 (queue_id=163 ✓ GOOD) but qids 162 and 164 have no queue_id-matched review (WIN-032 has multiple old cycles; SEC-2b.5 has SEC-2b.5.c1 at qid 155 and SEC-2b.5-post-apr18.c1 at qid 161 — neither is 164). So at least 2 additional likely-unreviewed pops in the same pattern on this date.

orthogonal_finding_cycle_numbering_backw.observation: SEC-4.1.c3.json reviewed_at 2026-04-18T07:42:00Z with cycle=3 and queue_id=158. SEC-4.1.c4.json reviewed_at 2026-04-18T05:12:46Z with cycle=4 and queue_id=163. c4 was written 2.5 hours BEFORE c3 but claims a higher cycle number.
orthogonal_finding_cycle_numbering_backw.likely_cause: Curmudgeon determines the next cycle by scanning reviews/ for the highest existing cycle for the target_id, then incrementing. Two concurrent (or stale-clone) curmudgeon runs against SEC-4.1 can produce out-of-order cycle numbers: run A with an old clone reads reviews up to c2, decides next is c3, writes c3; run B with a newer clone reads up to c2 + c3 (if A wrote fast) or up to c3 directly, writes c4. If A's clone was stale AND A's run took a long time (start at 03:20 run, finish at 07:42Z), A writes c3 AFTER B's c4 has landed. Aggravating factor: the 07:42Z review's claimed queue_id=158 was popped 2026-04-17T23:26Z (yesterday evening) — so this is an eight-hour-delayed review against an item that should not have been under review anymore. Run A started against qid 158 before the overnight churn, was slow, and landed 8 hours late.
orthogonal_finding_cycle_numbering_backw.scope_classification: ORTHOGONAL to this PROP. Fixing the pop/skip substring bug does not repair cycle numbering. The cycle fix is about curmudgeon's cycle-computation race (needs either atomic cycle allocation in a counter file, or reviewed_at-based ordering that ignores cycle numbers entirely). Flagged here for a separate PROP — recommend tinker take this on a future run if the pattern repeats.
orthogonal_finding_cycle_numbering_backw.severity_if_unfixed: moderate — misleads future readers about review chronology and can cause mistaken triage, but does not corrupt review content itself.

proposed_fix.summary: Replace the decider's Step E2 pop filter and the curmudgeon's Step 0b Procedure line 2 skip filter with a precondition that requires one of: (a) a review file whose queue_id field exactly matches the queue item's queue_id (strict); or (b) a review file whose target_id substring-matches AND whose reviewed_at post-dates the queue item's pushed_at (soft fallback for legacy reviews without queue_id tags). If neither matches, do NOT pop / do NOT skip. Also add a stale-item escalation: if a queue item has been in the queue >7 days without popping, the decider writes an attention-inbox entry and leaves the item in place (no auto-pop on staleness). The new rule gives operator-pushed items an escape hatch: if pushed_by contains 'operator' AND the item has field require_matching_review_file:false, the old substring match applies (backward-compatible for operator scripts that don't tag queue_ids on reviews).

files_to_modify.explanation: Adds the PROP-009 precondition marker, pre-parses review JSON once per run, evaluates strict (queue_id) / soft (reviewed_at vs pushed_at) / operator-bypass conditions, and runs a shadow log during migration. The `enforce` flag is an env var (default false for shadow). Flip to true after 3 days of shadow audit.

files_to_modify.find: 2. Find the **first un-reviewed** item in `queue` — strict FIFO, no reordering. To determine if an item has already been reviewed, check whether a review file exists in **`${WORKSPACE}/monitor/curmudgeon/reviews/`** (NOT the clone — you write reviews to the workspace, and workspace-sync may not have pushed them to git yet; the clone would be missing your own recent reviews). Check if any filename contains the item's `target_id`. For example, queue item `target_id: "WIN-003"` is already reviewed if `WIN-003.c2.json` (or any file matching `WIN-003*`) exists. Queue item `target_id: "EXP-050"` is already reviewed if `EXP-050-proposal.c2.json` exists. For `EXP-054-batch`, check for `EXP-054-batch*.json`. For section items like `part3-3.1b`, check for `SEC-3.1b*.json`. **If ALL queue items have matching review files, skip to Step 0c** — the queue is fully reviewed and the decider will clean it up on its next run.
files_to_modify.replace:
2. Find the **first un-reviewed** item in `queue` — strict FIFO, no reordering. **PROP-009 precondition:** an item is 'already reviewed' ONLY if one of the following holds (read from `${WORKSPACE}/monitor/curmudgeon/reviews/`, not the clone):
   - **Strict:** a review file exists whose `queue_id` field equals this item's `queue_id`; OR
   - **Soft fallback (for legacy reviews without queue_id tags):** a review file exists whose filename substring-matches the `target_id` AND whose `reviewed_at` timestamp post-dates this item's `pushed_at` timestamp.

   Substring-match alone is NOT sufficient — a pre-existing old review for the same `target_id` does not count. For example, queue item `target_id: "WIN-058"` with `queue_id: 166` and `pushed_at: "2026-04-18T07:26Z"` is NOT covered by `WIN-058.c2.json` from 2026-04-08, even though the filename substring-matches. You MUST review this item.

   When you DO write a review for a queue-sourced item, **always include the `queue_id` field** inside the review JSON, copied verbatim from the queue item — this is how the decider's Step E2 pop filter finds your work. Do not rely on the filename alone to carry queue identity. Cycle-number determination (`.c2`, `.c3`, `.c4`) should still follow the existing rule (highest existing cycle + 1), but the `queue_id` field disambiguates which push this review covers.

   For section items like `part3-3.1b`, the search-terms expansion (`SEC-3.1b*`) still applies to the substring portion of the soft fallback.

   **If ALL queue items either (a) strict-match or (b) soft-match under the above rule, skip to Step 0c** — the queue is fully reviewed and the decider will clean it up on its next run.
files_to_modify.explanation: Mirrors the decider Step E2 precondition on the curmudgeon side. Prevents curmudgeon from falsely treating a freshly pushed post-integration re-review as already-reviewed because an old cycle-2 review file exists. Also adds the explicit requirement that curmudgeon include queue_id in its review output — this is the clean way to close the loop.

migration_plan.recommended: 3-day shadow period starting on patch land (decider Step E2 logs would-have-blocked items to monitor/integrity/prop-009-shadow.jsonl but still pops them). Tinker audits the shadow log on its daily Mode 2 runs to confirm the rate of would-be-blocks is in the 2-5/day range expected and that no legitimate reviews are being misclassified. After 3 clean days, set PROP_009_ENFORCE=true in the decider's scheduled-task environment (or hardcode `const enforce=true;` in the node block). Rollback: revert the find/replace block in a single commit; no data migration needed because the shadow log is append-only and the history schema is backward-compatible (the new `pop_reason` field is additive).
migration_plan.alternative_considered_immediate_enforce: REJECTED — immediate enforcement risks creating a queue backlog if curmudgeon's first run after patch doesn't include queue_id in its review output (the curmudgeon.md patch needs to land simultaneously, AND the next curmudgeon run has to actually execute and include queue_id). Shadow period lets us confirm the curmudgeon side is also working before we make pops strict.
migration_plan.curmudgeon_side_coordination: Ship both prompt patches in the same commit. The decider side's shadow is safe even if curmudgeon is slow to adopt the queue_id tagging, because the soft fallback (reviewed_at > pushed_at) covers reviews that don't include queue_id.
audit_criteria_before_flipping_enforce: Shadow log shows <=5 would-have-blocked items per day on average.
Every would-have-blocked item corresponds to a legitimately-unreviewed push (not a pop that should have happened).
At least one successful end-to-end cycle has occurred where curmudgeon writes queue_id into a review and the decider strict-matches it (verify via pop_reason: 'strict_queue_id' in history).

tradeoffs: GAIN: post-integration adversarial reviews are no longer silently skipped — SEC-4.1, WIN-058, WIN-062 class regressions (publishing a major rewrite with no second opinion) are blocked at the gate instead of detected 24-48h later. LOSE: one extra JSON.parse per review file per decider E2 run (50-200 files × ~5KB each = trivial, <100ms). RISK: shadow-period false negatives — the soft fallback treats a review whose reviewed_at post-dates pushed_at as sufficient even if the review was actually a separate unrelated cycle. This is intentional (legacy reviews without queue_id need a working fallback) and bounded: for it to cause a bad pop, the curmudgeon must write a review at a time that coincidentally post-dates a push, without actually knowing about the push. In practice this is rare because pushed_at is when the decider stages the push and a concurrent curmudgeon run sees the updated queue. RISK: curmudgeon runs with a stale clone may write reviews with out-of-order cycle numbers that carry the right queue_id (still valid for the pop gate). Cycle-number ordering is a separate issue (see orthogonal finding).

estimated_impact.token_cost: negligible — decider Step E2 is 4-6× longer but runs once per decider invocation (~5 times/day). Net: <100 extra lines of Opus context per day.
estimated_impact.quality_gain: critical — closes the post-integration adversarial review gap. Directly prevents the SEC-4.1/WIN-058 class regression documented in the directive.
estimated_impact.risk_reduction: high — the current bug is silent (no error, no log, no alert) and was only detected because the operator noticed 10 pops without corresponding reviews during manual audit. Shadow log creates observability for future regressions in this pathway.

why_human_needed: Operator must (a) approve the precondition wording (strict queue_id + soft reviewed_at > pushed_at + operator bypass), (b) approve the 3-day shadow migration vs immediate enforcement, (c) decide whether to open a separate follow-up PROP for the SEC-4.1 cycle-numbering-backwards anomaly or treat as known-acceptable, (d) land the decider and curmudgeon patches in a single commit so they deploy together.
verification_pattern: grep -q 'PROP-009 precondition' monitor/prompts/decider.md && grep -q 'PROP-009 precondition' monitor/prompts/curmudgeon.md && echo FIXED || echo STILL_BROKEN

related.prior_proposal_non_overlapping: PROP-008 (change-detection cooldown + magnitude gates — orthogonal; operates on Step 0d change-driven scans, not priority-queue post-integration pushes)
related_commits: e4630ec (2026-04-18T09:41Z) — decider queue-management commit; popped qids 170-174 (same file shows pushed_at=09:34Z → popped_at=09:41Z, 7 min window)
related.orthogonal_pending_followup: SEC-4.1 cycle-numbering race (c3 at 07:42Z with cycle=3 written AFTER c4 at 05:12Z with cycle=4). Recommend tinker pick up as a separate PROP if pattern repeats in next 7 days; likely root cause is curmudgeon cycle-computation race across overlapping runs or stale clones.
